text: And she can no longer do her activities of daily living.
She can't play the flute.
So we start with a surgical correction of this.
This is an incision above the clavicle.
Here you can see as we're raising subplatysmal flaps, we're starting to expose the area where there's the problem.
We gradually expose the scalene fat pad.
We meticulously take this down and mobilize this laterally.
It's very important to tie off the branches here so we don't get a lymph leak.
This exposes the anterior scalene muscle and the phrenic nerve, and you see us here carefully dissecting the phrenic nerve off the anterior scalene muscle.

This is one of the muscles that causes the compression, so now we're resecting the anterior scalene muscle off of the first rib, hopefully relieve the compression and relieve her symptoms of arm pain.
Here you see the anterior scalene muscle coming up, and now it's out.
Now we do a meticulous neurolysis of the brachial plexus.
You can see as we're removing all of the scar around these very important nerves to the arm.
It's very important that this be done precisely and meticulously to get a good result in terms of relief of pain and dysfunction.
And you can see us here, there's a lot of scar in her arm.
thoracic outlet.
This is from the chronic compression that's been occurring for years and we're removing all of that scar to free up the nerves nicely and to make sure that the arm works as well as it can.
We then identify the long thoracic nerve, which you see here behind the brachial plexus, as well as free up all four of the cords.
The long thoracic nerve is the posterior limit of the dissection.
It's where we divide the rib at the back, and you see it here pointed out by the forceps.
We then take out the middle scalene muscle.
At this point, the
Scalene muscles in the rib have been removed and the brachial plexus you see here is nice and free.
There's no scar that you saw before.
Here's the rib piece that we took out.
It's actually very small.
Your ribs are quite small at the top of your rib cage.
There's the anterior scalene muscle and the last piece we removed was the middle scalene muscle and those are what pull the rib up and constrict the space and give these symptoms in the arm.
And so we've removed all of those pieces.
We now show you the subclavian artery, which is also not compressed.
And then all the cords of the brachial plexus
And so here we see all the structures, all the scar has been removed, the entire area is free of the compression.
And this is going to result in a good functional outcome in this arm and hopefully return her to be able to playing the flute and doing her activities of daily living.
We check everything.
We check all four of the cords.
And now you see us close.
We re-approximate the scalene fat pad back to its original state.
anatomical position medially and then we place a soft suction drain to prevent and contain any lymph leak.
We usually take this out in a day and then we close this in multiple anatomical layers to give a nice precise and cosmetically attractive closure.
Patients usually spend one to three days in the hospital and can return to normal activity within several days.
We recommend physical therapy afterwards, and we've had excellent results with patients that have been completely disabled now able to use their arm and re-engage their activities and exercise.
